text: The anchoring effect reveals how effortlessly System 1 contaminates deliberate judgment. When people are asked to estimate an unknown quantity — say, the age of a historical figure — after first being exposed to an arbitrary number, their estimates cluster near that anchor, even when they know it is irrelevant. Kahneman and Tversky showed that this bias persists even among experts and even when the anchor is transparently absurd, such as a number generated by spinning a wheel. System 2 adjusts from the anchor but typically stops too early, leaving residue from a value that should have been ignored entirely.
question: According to the passage, why does anchoring persist even when the anchor number is obviously irrelevant?

options.text: People deliberately use anchors as reference points
System 2 fully corrects for the anchor but introduces new errors
System 2 adjusts from the anchor but typically stops before reaching the correct value
System 1 is unable to process numerical information
